meta.title: 5th Bulletin of the Grande Armée, 1806 series (Jena-Auerstedt)

lexical_units.gloss_notes: French `gloire` carries military fame, radiance, public renown, and honor; do not flatten it into generic English glory.
French `honneur` in military bulletin register links reputation, obligation, and public esteem.

lexical_units.basic_meaning_source: Littre / Dictionnaire de l'Academie francaise, historical sense
lexical_units.contextual_meaning: battle death and bloodshed are framed as produced, sown, or converted into glory-bearing cost
lexical_units.comparison_basis: Battlefield violence is understood through production, sowing, and sacrificial blood cost.
lexical_units.contrast_explanation: Some death is literal, but the phrasing treats death as an output, seed, or glory-bearing sacrificial cost.
lexical_units.review_notes: Preserve source-language `mort`/`sang`; English death/blood glosses are only aids. Gallica OCR noise is visible in the local sentence; use the French span_text as reviewed source text and verify before quotation.
lexical_units.gloss_notes: French `mort` and `sang` may be literal while still serving sacrifice/glory framing.
French `victoire` may name an event, a result, or a quasi-agent in personified phrasing.

lexical_units.basic_meaning_source: Littre / Dictionnaire de l'Academie francaise, historical sense
lexical_units.contextual_meaning: `victoire` is treated as an agent, possession, or hesitant outcome in battle narrative
lexical_units.comparison_basis: Battle outcome is understood through personification and possession.
lexical_units.basic_meaning: a military success or winning event
lexical_units.contrast_explanation: Victory is abstract but is described as hesitating, deciding, or belonging to an army.
lexical_units.review_notes: French `victoire` is marked only where the local phrasing gives it agency or object status. Gallica OCR noise is visible in the local sentence; use the French span_text as reviewed source text and verify before quotation.
lexical_units.gloss_notes: French `victoire` may name an event, a result, or a quasi-agent in personified phrasing.
French `morts` may be literal battlefield dead or memorialized sacrificial dead.
French `morts` may be literal battlefield dead or memorialized sacrificial dead.
